lesson.menu_desc: Get buy-in from people who do not report to you: find the win for them, get them to act through framing rather than orders, enlist an ally before the meeting, and rally support around a shared goal — using causative structures (get someone to / have someone do) and purpose clauses (so that / in order to) to move the org without pulling rank — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 — Influencing Without Authority — Getting Buy-In Across the Org
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 16 — Influencing Without Authority

listenings.text: Okay, think this through before the cross-functional meeting. I need three teams on board for the regional rollout, and not one of them reports to me. I cannot order anyone. So if I walk in and just announce the plan, I will get polite nods and zero follow-through. Here is the plan to actually win them over. First, I find the win for each of them before I ask for anything. For finance, the angle is a cleaner forecast; for IT, fewer last-minute fire drills; for operations, a workload they can plan around. People back what helps them, so I lead with their benefit, not mine. Second, I do not give orders, I frame. Instead of saying do this, I get them to see it as their own idea. I ask what would have to be true for this to work for your team, so that they shape the how and own the outcome. Third, I never go in cold. I enlist one ally first, usually the person with the most credibility in the room, and I get them to champion the idea early. One advocate in the room is worth ten slides. Fourth, I tie everything to a goal we already share, so that it stops being my project and becomes our project. And I remember reciprocity: I show up for their priorities first, and support comes back later. Find the win, frame it, enlist an ally, rally around the shared goal. That is how you move the org when you cannot pull rank.

Hi Victor, it's Renata. Quick thought before your cross-functional push, because influencing without authority is the skill that separates a manager from a leader, and most people get it backwards. Three things. First, stop trying to be convincing and start being useful. Before you ask anyone for anything, work out what they actually want and lead with that. People do not act because your logic is strong; they act because they see what is in it for them. Find the win for the other side first, every time. Second, do not push, pull. You cannot order a peer, so do not try. Get them to arrive at the conclusion themselves by asking the right questions, so that the plan feels like theirs, not yours. A plan someone owns gets done; a plan someone was told to follow quietly dies. Third, and this is where strong managers slip, never walk into the room cold. Enlist one credible ally beforehand and have them champion the idea, so the momentum is already there when you arrive. And bank goodwill early through reciprocity. Support other people's priorities before you need theirs, and they will rally to yours when it counts. Be useful, pull instead of push, line up an advocate, and tie it to a goal you both share. Do that and you will move the whole org without a single line on the org chart.
